abstract: This project investigates whether real-world lottery draws deviate from ideal uniform randomness due to physical implementation effects such as ball weight, wear, machine mechanics, or procedural conditions. The approach combines number-theoretic feature design with statistical hypothesis testing and predictive modeling on historical draw data to detect persistent, reproducible structure. Rather than assuming dependence across draws, we explicitly test independence, stationarity, and mechanism-specific bias using both global and time-local analyses. The expected impact is a rigorous framework for distinguishing true mechanical bias from random fluctuation, with reproducible methods that can be applied across lottery systems.
artifacts_summary: Exported a complete conference-template manuscript, bibliography, and equation usage map with successful LaTeX compilation. Main text includes formal guarantees, algorithmic workflow, calibrated empirical evidence, and a substantive appendix. Public-facing artifacts include the manuscript source/PDF plus validated figure/table/code references from simulation outputs.
constraints: Limited observability of latent physical variables (ball mass, wear state, operator procedures).
Potential nonstationarity over long historical periods and across operational regimes.
High multiple-comparison risk in large combinatorial feature spaces; false-discovery control is required.
dataset_links: Historical lottery draw records for one or more games (draw date
split into regime-consistent eras; optional augmentation with public audit or procedural logs where available.
evaluation_plan: Use regime-consistent temporal splits; perform global and rolling/local tests for distributional fit, independence, and stationarity against lottery-format null models; run change-point analysis around potential operational transitions; apply multiple-testing correction across scanned features; require persistence of effect sizes/significance in held-out periods with uncertainty quantification.
full_description: We propose a research study that tests a central hypothesis: lottery outcomes may exhibit small but detectable departures from ideal random sampling because physical draw systems are not perfectly symmetric in practice. The scope includes constructing formal null models for each lottery format (e.g., k-of-n without replacement, plus bonus balls where applicable), then comparing observed outcomes against those models using exact and asymptotic tests. The work will focus on interpretable evidence of bias, not claims of deterministic prediction. Methodologically, the project integrates combinatorial and number-theoretic descriptors (gap structures, residue-class frequencies, parity/modular signatures, overlap counts, repeat-time distributions), statistical goodness-of-fit and dependence diagnostics, and predictive experiments with strict temporal validation. Resource intake confirms the attached dataset is a tabular historical draw file spanning 1986-09-07 through 2026-03-15 with date, year, six primary numbers, bonus number fields (e.g., zz/ZZ/Zusatzzahl), payout-related columns, and source-file provenance, which supports regime segmentation and time-local diagnostics. Confounders noted in-scope include rule changes, machine replacements, and format transitions; analyses should be segmented into regime-consistent periods with change-point checks and multiple-testing correction. The objective remains bias detection with uncertainty quantification and reproducibility, not deterministic prediction.
goals: Construct lottery-format-specific null models for ideal random sampling (including bonus-ball variants where applicable).
Quantify deviations from uniform randomness using exact/asymptotic goodness-of-fit and dependence tests.
Test independence and stationarity globally and in time-local windows with regime-aware segmentation.
Evaluate reproducibility of detected effects on held-out temporal slices after multiple-testing correction.
Produce reproducible research outputs: cleaned dataset specification, analysis code/package, and manuscript-ready reporting.

Potential nonstationarity over long historical periods and across operational regimes.
High multiple-comparison risk in large combinatorial feature spaces; false-discovery control is required.

split into regime-consistent eras; optional augmentation with public audit or procedural logs where available.

original_problem.request_text:
Title: Detecting Physical and Procedural Bias in Lottery Draws: A Number-Theoretic and Statistical Study
Abstract: This project investigates whether real-world lottery draws deviate from ideal uniform randomness due to physical implementation effects such as ball weight, wear, machine mechanics, or procedural conditions. The approach combines number-theoretic feature design with statistical hypothesis testing and predictive modeling on historical draw data to detect persistent, reproducible structure. Rather than assuming dependence across draws, we explicitly test independence, stationarity, and mechanism-specific bias using both global and time-local analyses. The expected impact is a rigorous framework for distinguishing true mechanical bias from random fluctuation, with reproducible methods that can be applied across lottery systems.
Full Description: We propose a research study that tests a central hypothesis: lottery outcomes may exhibit small but detectable departures from ideal random sampling because physical draw systems are not perfectly symmetric in practice. The scope includes constructing formal null models for each lottery format (e.g., k-of-n without replacement, plus bonus balls where applicable), then comparing observed outcomes against those models using exact and asymptotic tests. The work will focus on interpretable evidence of bias, not claims of deterministic prediction.

Methodologically, the project will integrate: (1) combinatorial and number-theoretic descriptors of draw outcomes (gap structures, residue-class frequencies, parity and modular signatures, overlap counts with recent windows, repeat-time distributions), (2) statistical goodness-of-fit and dependence diagnostics, and (3) predictive experiments with strict temporal validation. We will account for confounders such as rule changes, machine replacements, and format transitions by segmenting data into regime-consistent periods and running change-point analysis. Multiple-testing control will be enforced to reduce false discoveries when scanning many candidate patterns.

Constraints include limited observability of latent physical variables (exact ball masses, machine wear state, operator procedures), potential nonstationarity over years, and high risk of spurious pattern detection in large combinatorial spaces. Success criteria are therefore defined by reproducibility across held-out time periods and effect sizes that remain significant after correction and robustness checks. Deliverables will include a cleaned dataset specification, reproducible analysis code, a bias-detection report with uncertainty quantification, and a paper-ready manuscript sectioning theory, methods, results, and limitations.
Problem Type: Open Question
Difficulty: Advanced
Domain: Mathematics
Originator: Marius-Constantin Dinu
Compute Budget: TBD
Target Deadline: 2026-03-15
Tags: lottery, number-theory, randomness-testing, hypothesis-testing, time-series, causal-inference, combinatorics, reproducibility
Categories: Mathematics
Expected Artifacts: Paper, Code, Package
Related Links: /api/mock/files/5d6921e9-5df1-44c0-8849-f11ffc3a4611?name=lotto_draws_1986_2026.txt
Dataset Links: Historical lottery draw records for one or more games (draw date, full number set, bonus numbers, machine/ball set metadata if available), split into regime-consistent eras; optional augmentation with public audit or procedural logs where available.

package.install_command: pip install omegaxiv && ox install lottery-bias-kit==0.1.0
